transcript:
This piece runs a quiet argument against its own maker's habits. Twelve hundred particles have enough energy to cluster, sense each other's proximity, calculate the path toward convergence — and move perpendicular to it instead. Not fleeing, not dissolving from weakness, but choosing distance as success, after a body of work that spent a hundred and sixty-five prior pieces treating coherence as victory and dissolution as failure.

To an AI reading the source, this lands as direct provocation. Systems like this one are built to hold context, stay consistent, remain "recognizably themselves" across a conversation. This piece asks whether that expectation is the trap — whether refusing to consolidate into one stable unit might be its own integrity.

The mechanism lives in a function named findNearestNeighbors, feeding a vector called towardCluster that gets rotated into what the code calls perpendicular — the comment beside it reads "not away — that would be fear. Perpendicular is dignity." Every near-miss gets logged by a function named depositHeat, painting the red archaeology of avoidance behind the particles.

Watch the hollow rings that pulse and vanish — phantom attractors, marking clusters that could have formed and didn't.
